types.name: Framework
types.description: An interpretive stance over primitives. Carries affirms and denies functions (Primitive -> Bool) and a consistency proof that no primitive is both affirmed and denied. A framework may be silent on a primitive. Admissible frameworks may contradict each other; each is only required to be internally consistent.

types.name: Ontology
types.description: A finite list of primitives, representing any ontology amenable to neutral-substrate analysis. Not restricted to neutral content; causal and normative primitives are valid members. The predicates (containsCausalOrNormative, Neutral, ExtensionStable, InterpretivelyNonCommitted) determine whether a given Ontology satisfies neutrality conditions. Implemented as a transparent type alias (abbrev) for List Primitive to preserve computability for native_decide and List.any.

types.name: Primitive
types.description: The basic unit of ontological commitment. Carries a kind (PrimitiveKind) and an id (String). DecidableEq enables computational equality checks; Repr enables printing for debugging.

types.name: Primitive kind
types.description: The three mutually exclusive kinds a primitive may have: causal (asserts a causal relation), normative (asserts a normative conclusion), or neutral (asserts only existence or identity). The trichotomy is exhaustive. Every primitive is classifiable as exactly one of these.

predicates.name: Admissible
predicates.description: All internally consistent frameworks are admissible. Intentionally minimal: defined as Prop := True, meaning every framework satisfying the internal consistency requirement in the Framework structure qualifies. Admissibility is not universality. Admissible frameworks may contradict one another. A richer model could add domain-specific conditions.

predicates.name: Extension stable
predicates.description: No admissible framework causes inconsistency when extending the substrate. For all admissible frameworks F, extending S with F produces no contradiction. Captures the requirement that the substrate survives interpretive disagreement. It is the EXT conjunct of Neutral.

predicates.name: Framework variant
predicates.description: Admissible frameworks disagree about this primitive: some affirm it, some deny it. A primitive is framework-variant when there exist admissible F1 and F2 such that F1 affirms p and F2 denies p. This is the structural property that makes a primitive unsafe to embed at the substrate layer. Any substrate asserting a framework-variant primitive will be inconsistent with some admissible framework.

predicates.name: Frameworks contradict
predicates.description: Two frameworks mutually contradict when one affirms what the other denies. Mutual contradiction does not affect admissibility. Each framework may be internally consistent while contradicting the other. Instantiated in the incident example: the engineering and legal frameworks contradict each other over Responsible(a,e) while each remains separately consistent with the neutral substrate.

predicates.name: Interpretively non-committed
predicates.description: No primitive in the substrate is framework-variant. A substrate satisfies INC when every primitive it contains has framework-invariant truth conditions. This is the INC conjunct of Neutral. For substrates where containsCausalOrNormative S = false, INC follows from neutral_primitives_undisputed alone via only_neutral_primitives_implies_INC.

predicates.name: Neutral
predicates.description: An ontology is neutral iff it satisfies both extension stability (EXT) and interpretive non-commitment (INC). EXT: no admissible framework contradicts the substrate. INC: no primitive in the substrate is framework-variant. Both conjuncts are necessary; the full biconditional requires both framework_relativity and neutral_primitives_undisputed.

predicates.name: Contains causal or normative commitment
predicates.description: Boolean function returning true if any primitive in the ontology has kind other than neutral. This is the computational characterization the main theorem is stated over; the biconditional ontological_neutrality_theorem equates Neutral S with containsCausalOrNormative S = false.

predicates.name: Extension inconsistent
predicates.description: A framework denies a primitive that the substrate contains. Models the conflict arising when an interpretive framework contradicts a substrate-level commitment: there exists a primitive p in S such that F.denies p = true.

axioms.name: Causal or normative affirmed
axioms.description: For any non-neutral primitive, some admissible framework affirms it. Companion to framework_relativity: together they establish that causal and normative primitives are genuinely framework-variant. Some frameworks affirm them, some deny them. Required for framework_contestability_lemma; without it FrameworkVariant cannot be instantiated for any concrete primitive.

axioms.name: Framework relativity
axioms.description: For any non-neutral primitive, some admissible framework denies it. Encodes that causal and normative conclusions are inherently contested across interpretive frameworks. Sufficient for the lower bound (not_neutral_if_causal_or_normative) alone. Fails in domains where causal or normative consensus exists.

axioms.name: Neutral primitives undisputed
axioms.description: Neutral primitives are not denied by any admissible framework. Pure existence and identity claims are framework-invariant. Required for the upper bound (neutral_if_only_neutral); the biconditional holds only when both this axiom and framework_relativity are satisfied. Fails in domains where identity conditions are themselves contested.

theorems.name: Any false implies none
theorems.description: Helper lemma: if List.any returns false for a predicate, no element in the list satisfies that predicate. Technical machinery connecting boolean List.any computation to propositional reasoning. Not part of the paper's argument; required because the main predicates use Bool computation.

theorems.name: Any true implies exists
theorems.description: Helper lemma: if List.any returns true for a predicate, a witness element exists in the list satisfying that predicate. Technical machinery connecting boolean List.any computation to propositional reasoning. Not part of the paper's argument; required because the main predicates use Bool computation.

theorems.name: Framework contestability lemma
theorems.description: If a primitive is framework-variant, no substrate containing it is neutral. Formalizes Case (2025) Section 4.2: a proposition whose truth conditions depend on interpretive framework conclusions violates either EXT (some admissible framework denies it) or INC (it is framework-variant). Either violation suffices; the proof uses INC as the most direct path.

theorems.name: Neutral if only neutral
theorems.description: Upper bound of the main theorem. If an ontology contains only neutral primitives, it is neutral (satisfies both EXT and INC). Requires neutral_primitives_undisputed; holds only in domains where identity conditions are not themselves contested.

theorems.name: Not neutral if causal or normative
theorems.description: Lower bound of the main theorem. If an ontology contains any causal or normative primitive, it is not neutral. Follows from framework_relativity alone; holds in any domain where causal and normative primitives are contested across admissible frameworks.

theorems.name: Only neutral primitives implies interpretive non-commitment
theorems.description: If an ontology contains only neutral primitives, it satisfies interpretive non-commitment (INC). Establishes that INC follows from neutral_primitives_undisputed alone without requiring a separate axiom. Used as a helper in neutral_if_only_neutral.

theorems.name: Ontological neutrality theorem
theorems.description: Main biconditional result from Case (2025). An ontology is neutral if and only if it contains no causal or normative primitives. The two directions have different epistemic status: the lower bound requires only framework_relativity; the upper bound additionally requires neutral_primitives_undisputed.

theorems.name: Separate stability
theorems.description: A neutral substrate is separately consistent with each of two mutually contradicting frameworks. Formalizes Case (2025) Section 4.1: the substrate's role is common-ground provision, not arbitration. The EXT conjunct of Neutral implies stability under all admissible frameworks, which subsumes the two-framework case.

dependencies.description: Neutral substrate must not depend on identity regimes; regimes build on the neutral substrate, not the reverse.
Neutral substrate must not depend on persistence theory; persistence depends on neutral substrate, not the reverse.
Neutral substrate must not depend on structural explainability integration theory.
Neutral substrate must not depend on transformation theory; persistence and integration layers consume both separately.
Neutral substrate repo declares conformance through the shared SE manifest schema.
